title: Black Box Data After a Semi Crash in Plano, Texas
twitter: Semi crash in Plano, TX? The truck's black box data — speed, braking, hours of service — can vanish fast if not preserved. Act NOW. Call 855-WRECKMATCH (855-897-3256) or visit https://www.wreckmatch.com/blog/black-box-data-after-a-semi-crash-in-plano-texas
linkedin:
If you handle trucking litigation or represent injury victims in Texas, black box data is one of the most powerful — and time-sensitive — pieces of evidence in any semi-truck accident case.

After a crash in Plano, Texas, a commercial truck's Electronic Control Module (ECM) and Electronic Logging Device (ELD) can capture critical data: vehicle speed at impact, hard braking events, hours of service violations, and even engine fault codes. This data doesn't last forever. Without a timely legal hold or spoliation letter, it can be overwritten within days.

WreckMatch connects injured victims with attorneys from our network of 800+ law firms nationwide who understand how to move fast on evidence preservation — and how to build a case that holds trucking companies accountable.

If your firm handles truck accident cases in Texas or you know someone who was hurt in a Plano semi crash, now is the time to act. The evidence window is narrow.

facebook:
If someone you love was hurt in a semi-truck crash in Plano, Texas, you may not realize that the truck itself was recording everything — speed, braking, driver hours, and more. That data lives inside the truck's black box, and it could be the key to proving what really happened.

The hard truth? That evidence can disappear quickly. Trucking companies and their insurers know how valuable it is, and without fast legal action, it can be overwritten or lost entirely.

You deserve answers — and the right attorney can send a legal hold letter immediately to protect that data before it's gone. Don't wait on this one. Every day matters.

reddit_title: Semi truck crash in Plano, TX — how do you preserve black box (ECM/ELD) data before it gets overwritten?
reddit_body:
If you or someone you know was involved in a semi-truck accident in Texas, one of the most important things to understand is that commercial trucks record a huge amount of data — speed, braking, throttle position, hours of service, and engine faults. This is stored in the ECM (Electronic Control Module) and ELD (Electronic Logging Device), often called the "black box."

The problem is that this data doesn't stick around forever. Depending on the system, it can be overwritten within days. The trucking company's insurer is usually working fast to protect their client — so you need to move just as fast. An attorney can send a spoliation letter or seek an emergency court order to preserve the data before it disappears.

If you're trying to figure out next steps, connecting with a truck accident attorney quickly is critical — not weeks from now, today. Sites like wreckmatch.com can help match you with an experienced attorney in your area who handles these cases and knows how to lock down evidence fast. Time is genuinely not on your side here.
